At 87, I have lived through many public health shifts, from polio to the recent pandemic, and I feel a particular responsibility to listen with patience rather than to judge. My faith teaches compassion, and this compassion guides me to approach these discussions with the same humility I practiced while photographing the city’s historic architecture. I remind myself that every person has a story—some have seen the early days of the vaccine rollout, while others have only heard rumors on the radio or in the corner of a shop. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: I often find myself walking through the quiet streets of Philadelphia, a gentle breeze lifting the leaves, and thinking about the conversations that swirl around our community about healthcare and COVID vaccines. At 87, I have lived through many public health shifts, from polio to the recent pandemic, and I feel a particular responsibility to listen with patience rather than to judge. My faith teaches compassion, and this compassion guides me to approach these discussions with the same humility I practiced while photographing the city’s historic architecture. I remind myself that every person has a story—some have seen the early days of the vaccine rollout, while others have only heard rumors on the radio or in the corner of a shop. When a friend expresses fear, I do not dismiss it as mere superstition; instead, I lean on credible sources like the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention or the World Health Organization, sharing PDFs I have saved on my phone, because I know that in the age of social media, misinformation can spread faster than a summer storm. Yet I also recognize that my own anxiety sometimes makes me overemphasize precautions. I try to balance that by saying, “I understand why you feel uneasy, and I would feel the same if I were in your shoes.” I bring in my Islamic perspective, noting that scholars have consistently encouraged seeking protection from illness. I do not push my beliefs; I share them gently, hoping to bridge understanding. I also remind my family that vaccination is a communal act—protecting grandchildren, my husband, and neighbors who cannot get vaccinated for medical reasons. In these conversations, I keep the tone calm and respectful, acknowledging uncertainty while offering hope. The key, I have learned, is to stay informed, to listen actively, and to address fears with empathy, because our shared history of faith and resilience can become the foundation for a healthier, united community.
